images.caption: Figure 1:Multi4Denables (1) high-quality, efficient dynamic scene reconstruction via competitive multi-level specialization, and (2) compact, high-accuracy 4D segmentation with fast inference.

Figure 2:Overview of the Multi4D pipeline.We employ a bottom-up training scheme that enables competitive allocation across multi-level Gaussian subsets through cross-set self-supervision.
After optimization, the persistent subset𝒢p\mathcal{G}_{p}is frozen and used for efficient downstream 4D segmentation.
Figure 3:Qualitative comparisons with baseline methods[41,47,22,2]on Technicolor dataset[33]. Our method shows consistently superior dynamic details modeling.

Figure 4:Qualitative comparisons on the Neu3D dataset[19]. State-of-the-art deformation-based methods[41,2]suffer from over-smoothing in regions with complex appearance changes (highlighted in red), while 4D-primitive approaches[47,22]exhibit broken geometry in fast-moving areas (highlighted in blue). Multi4D successfully mitigates both artifacts and consistently achieves the highest visual fidelity.

Figure 5:Qualitative results on NeRF-DS[46]. 4D-primitive-based methods[22,47]degrade heavily under monocular supervision, while Multi4D preserves coherent geometry and fine specular details via decoupled motion and appearance modeling.

Figure 6:Qualitative Evaluation of 4D Segmentation with TRASE[21]. From left to right, we visualize high-dimensional semantic feature renderings, 3D motion trajectories (using Co-tracker[12]as a point-based reference), and predicted 2D object masks.

Figure 7:Overview of the Multi4D framework.
The scene is decomposed into three Gaussian subsets: static (𝒢s\mathcal{G}_{s}), persistent dynamic (𝒢d\mathcal{G}_{d}), and transient (𝒢t\mathcal{G}_{t}).
These subsets are jointly optimized under a bottom-up, self-regularized multi-level allocation scheme through a shared renderer, while maintaining independent optimization and density-control dynamics.
This design enables competitive allocation and emergent subset specialization.
Numbers in the figure correspond to the section numbers in the main paper describing each core component.

Figure 8:Subset specialization produced by our multi-level competitive allocation strategy.
For each scene we visualize the ground truth, the full Multi4D render, individual renders of each subset, and the learned dynamic–static separation mask.
The static subset (𝒢s\mathcal{G}_{s}) reconstructs the background geometry, persistent Gaussians (𝒢d\mathcal{G}_{d}) maintain coherent dynamic structure and motion identity, while transient primitives (𝒢t\mathcal{G}_{t}) capture localized high-frequency residuals such as fine appearance changes or specular highlights.
Across Neu3D[19], Technicolor[33], and NeRF-DS[46], this multi-level subset specialization jointly models dynamic scenes, producing compact representations while preserving fine dynamic details, achieving consistently superior performance.

Figure 9:Self-supervised dynamic–static decomposition.
A learned dynamic probability mask separates moving actors from the static background without external supervision.
An additional opacity regularization (ℒα\mathcal{L}_{\alpha}) aligns the rendered dynamic alpha with the mask, suppressing dynamic opacity in static regions and enabling structural decoupling between persistent dynamics and static geometry during early training.

Figure 10:4D semantic segmentation pipeline.
After dynamic reconstruction, semantic features are learned on the optimized persistent Gaussian set using SAM masks and contrastive learning.
The rendered semantic features are clustered to obtain consistent object identities.
At inference, a text prompt (Grounding-DINO) or mask is associated with the first frame via depth unprojection, and the selected cluster is tracked across time through the deformation field to produce temporally consistent 4D segmentation.

Figure 12:Per-frame PSNR on Neu3D test views. Mean PSNR: STG first-frame init 32.94, STG all-frame init 33.58, FreeTimeGS first-frame init 32.95 (our reproduction; official code unavailable), and Multi4D first-frame init 34.17.
Figure 13:New-object success/failure cases on monocular datasets[32,52].
